title: VLA Foundry: A Unified Framework for Training Vision-Language-Action Models
abstract: 本文介绍VLA Foundry，一个开源框架，可在单一代码库中统一实现大语言模型（LLM）、视觉语言模型（VLM）和视觉语言动作模型（VLA）的训练。当前多数开源VLA项目专注于动作训练阶段，往往拼接不兼容的预训练流水线。VLA Foundry则提供统一的训练堆栈，实现从语言预训练到动作专家微调的端到端控制。该框架既支持从头训练，也支持调用Hugging Face上的预训练主干网络。为展示框架实用性，我们训练并发布两类模型：第一类通过LLM→VLM→VLA全流程从头训练，第二类基于预训练的Qwen3-VL主干网络构建。我们在开放数据、开源的LBM Eval仿真器上评估两类模型的闭环策略性能，同时对该仿真器及STEP分析工具进行了可用性改进以方便公众使用。在标准评估场景下，我们完全开源的从头训练模型性能与先前的闭源工作相当，而替换为Qwen3-VL主干后，获得的强大多任务桌面操作策略在各项指标上大幅超越基线模型。VLA Foundry代码库已发布于https://github.com/TRI-ML/vla_foundry，所有多任务模型权重已在https://huggingface.co/collections/TRI-ML/vla-foundry 开源，更多定性演示视频请访问项目官网https://tri-ml.github.io/vla_foundry。

VLA Foundry: A Unified Framework for Training Vision-Language-Action Models
Jean Mercat
Sedrick Keh

Abstract
We present VLA Foundry, an open-source framework that unifies LLM, VLM, and VLA training in a single codebase. Most open-source VLA efforts specialize on the action training stage, often stitching together incompatible pretraining pipelines. VLA Foundry instead provides a shared training stack with end-to-end control, from language pretraining to action-expert fine-tuning. VLA Foundry supports both from-scratch training and pretrained backbones from Hugging Face. To demonstrate the utility of our framework, we train and release two types of models: the first trained fully from scratch through our LLM
→
\rightarrow
VLM
→
\rightarrow
VLA pipeline and the second built on the pretrained Qwen3-VL
[
bai2025qwen3
]
backbone. We evaluate closed-loop policy performance of both models on LBM Eval
[
lbm_eval2025
]
, an open-data, open-source simulator. We also contribute usability improvements to the simulator and the STEP
[
snyder2025step
]
analysis tools for easier public use.
In the nominal evaluation setting, our fully-open from-scratch model is on par with our prior closed-source work
[
lbmtri2025
]
and substituting in the Qwen3-VL backbone leads to a strong multi-task table top manipulation policy outperforming our baseline by a wide margin.
The VLA Foundry codebase is available at
https://github.com/TRI-ML/vla_foundry
and all multi-task model weights are released on
https://huggingface.co/collections/TRI-ML/vla-foundry
. Additional qualitative videos are available on the project website
https://tri-ml.github.io/vla_foundry
.
\correspondence
Jean Mercat and Katherine Liu at
\metadata
[Project page]
https://tri-ml.github.io/vla_foundry/
1
Introduction
Robotics foundation models are advancing at a rapid pace, with many systems
[
black2025pi05
,
intelligence2025pi06star
,
shukor2025smolvla
,
molmoact2025
,
dreamzero_ye2026worldactionmodelszeroshot
,
lin2026holobrain
]
demonstrating capabilities that would have seemed out of reach just a few years ago. As the frontier moves faster, the tooling required to support rigorous research must keep pace.
Many high-impact questions – about data scaling, backbone pretraining, and the interplay between robotics and non-robotics data – require both scale (compute, data, etc.), as well as modular algorithmic infrastructure that allow users full control over different parts of the model and training pipeline.
However, most existing codebases have either not been extensively tested at scale
[
galahad2025vlascratch
]
, or are largely focused on model releases
[
molmoact2025
,
physicalintelligence2025openpi
,
gr00tn1_2025
]
and therefore tightly coupled to specific algorithmic decisions, limiting research flexibility.
At the same time, data scarcity remains a fundamental bottleneck in robotics. Robot interaction data is severely constrained relative to data used for language and vision models, especially in diversity and in signal density per token
[
bandaru2025foundation
]
. As robot policies continue to scale, the relative importance of non-robotics data only grows
[
lin2026systematic
]
. Despite this data disparity, most open-source VLA frameworks focus narrowly on the action training stage, treating the upstream data recipe as fixed or out-of-scope. Such separation is problematic: data decisions made during LLM and VLM pretraining have direct consequences for downstream robotics performance. Exploring the design space requires a framework that treats the entire pipeline, from pretraining to policy learning, as a single, controllable system.
We developed
VLA Foundry
to address these challenges. VLA Foundry is a unified, open-source framework with a shared data-loading and training stack that spans LLM, VLM, and VLA training in a single codebase, giving practitioners control over the entire pipeline – from backbone pretraining to action-expert fine-tuning. Because every stage shares the same infrastructure, researchers can co-train across modalities, mix datasets, and prototype new architectures without stitching together disparate tools. The framework natively supports pretrained backbones from Hugging Face, and its modular, configuration-driven design lets users swap architectures, data pipelines, and training recipes through simple command-line or YAML changes.
VLA Foundry has the following key features:
•
Full pipeline controllability
, enabling researchers to intervene at any
stage of the data and training recipe — from backbone pretraining to action expert
fine-tuning — through a shared, configuration-driven interface.
•
Flexible multi-modal training
with probabilistic dataset mixing and dataloaders that support text, image-caption, and robotics data, allowing precise control over the training mixture at every stage.
•
Native Hugging Face integration
facilitates loading of pretrained vision encoder, LLM, and VLM backbones, making benchmarking new architectures straightforward within the same training and evaluation pipeline.
•
Scalable distributed training
built on FSDP2 and cloud-native tooling
(AWS SageMaker, S3), supporting multi-node, multi-GPU runs with automatic gradient
accumulation, mixed precision, and checkpoint synchronization.
Figure 1
:
VLA Foundry overview.
Unified and Configurable LLM-VLM-VLA Pipeline
: VLA Foundry was designed to enable flexible model composition. For example, users can train an LLM, use the LLM to train a VLM, and use the VLM to train a VLA.
Bootstrap off Pre-trained Models
: VLA Foundry also supports loading off-the-shelf VLMs from Hugging Face.
Pre-trained Open Models
: We release LLM, VLM, and VLA models trained both from scratch and finetuned from open weights under a permissive license at
https://huggingface.co/collections/TRI-ML/vla-foundry
.
2
Related Work
2.1
LLM/VLM Training Frameworks
Large Language Models (LLMs) form the foundation of modern multimodal systems, providing scalable sequence modeling capabilities, strong linguistic representations, and emergent reasoning abilities. Early work established the effectiveness of the scaling of transformer-based language models while subsequent efforts have largely focused on improving training efficiency, transparency, and reproducibility. Projects such as Megatron-LM
[
megatron-lm
]
, DeepSpeed
[
rasley2020deepspeed
]
, and GPT Neox
[
gpt-neox-library
]
introduced distributed training strategies that enabled scaling to hundreds of billions of parameters. More recent and accessible open training initiatives, including OpenLM
[
open_lm
]
, Olmo
[
olmo20242olmo2furious
]
, LLM360
[
liu2023llm360
]
, and its follow-up K2 model
[
liu2025k2
]
emphasize full stack transparency by releasing training data, code, intermediate checkpoints, and logs. Complementary efforts such as FastLLM
[
fastllm2024
]
provide practical recipes for training competitive models under more constrained compute budgets, while educational repositories such as nanoGPT
[
karpathy2022nanogpt
]
and LLMs from scratch
[
build-llms-from-scratch-book
]
have further lowered the barrier to reproducing end-end-end LLM training pipelines. Dataset frameworks such as DCLM
[
li2024datacomp
]
and FineWeb
[
penedo2024fineweb
]
provide high quality language datasets. Together, these works highlight a shift towards reproducible and modular LLM training frameworks.
Vision-language model (VLM) frameworks must additionally address cross-modal representation learning and heterogeneous data integration. A common and prominent design pattern in VLMs is to couple a pretrained vision encoder with language model backbone, with intermediate modules responsible for aligning visual and text representations. Frameworks such as OpenFlamingo
[
awadalla2023openflamingo
]
operationalize this approach by providing infrastructure for interleaved image-text sequence construction, multimodal batching, and various architecture choices that enable the training of autoregressive VLMs on web-scale data. Similarly, LLaVA
[
liu2023visual
]
offers a streamlined pipeline for multimodal instruction tuning, including data formatting, visual feature extraction, and supervised fine-tuning stages.
Other frameworks emphasize modularity and composability as first-class design principles. BLIP-2
[
li2023blip2
]
introduces a modular bridging component (Q-former) that decouples vision and language backbones, allowing each to be reused independently. Prismatic VLMs
[
karamcheti2024prismatic
]
extend this idea by explicitly structuring the framework around interchangeable components, enabling controlled experimentation over vision encoders, language models, and training mixtures. InternVL
[
chen2024internvl
]
further demonstrates how such modular designs can be scaled, incorporating large vision encoders and staged alignment strategies within a unified pipeline. Qwen
[
Qwen-VL
,
bai2025qwen3
]
offers state-of-the-art VLM capabilities in an open-source codebase. Complementing this line of work that primarily focuses on model architecture, dataset frameworks such as DataComp
[
gadre2023datacomp
]
provide standardized pipelines for construction and evaluating large-scale image-text datasets, addressing a critical bottleneck in reproducible multimodal training.
Across LLM and VLM settings, these frameworks expose several key dimensions of design including data pipelines (e.g. pre-tokenized vs. dynamic processing), model composition (e.g. monolithic vs. modular architectures), and training orchestration (e.g. distribution execution, staged vs joint optimization). As a result, existing systems span a spectrum from highly optimized distributed training backends to more modular, research oriented frameworks that facilitate experimentation with model architecture and data mixtures.
2.2
VLA Training Frameworks
In recent years, the open source vision-language-action (VLA) ecosystem has expanded rapidly, moving from a small number of isolated model releases to a broader set of training pipelines, pre-trained checkpoints and reproducible research frameworks. One of the earliest milestones of this shift was OpenVLA
[
kim2024openvla
]
which released a 7B-parameter model with a full PyTorch compatible codebase built off Prismatic
[
karamcheti2024prismatic
]
. Since then, a number of open-source alternatives have emerged. OpenPi
[
physicalintelligence2025openpi
]
provides training and fine-tuning support for Physical Intelligence’s
π
0
\pi_{0}
model series, with base checkpoints pretrained on more than 10,000 hours of robot data. GR00T
[
gr00tn1_2025
]
pairs a vision-language backbone with a diffusion transformer action head in a dual-system architecture trained on real, simulated, and synthetic data. MolmoAct
[
molmoact2025
]
explores a complementary direction by introducing an “Action Reasoning Model” that reasons in 3D space via depth-aware perception tokens rather than purely language-based action representations.
Beyond individual model development, several efforts have focused on standardization, infrastructure, and reproducibility of the entire VLA pipeline. LeRobot
[
cadene2024lerobot
]
adopts a community-first approach, emphasizing affordable hardware (SO-100/101 arms), integrating dataset collection, training, and deployment across affordable hardware platforms and lowering the barrier to real-world experimentation. They report results on a 450M-parameter model, SmolVLA
[
shukor2025smolvla
]
, which is trained on a single GPU and remains competitive with much larger VLAs on standard benchmarks. VLAb
[
aubakirova2025vlab
]
complements LeRobot as a dedicated pretraining library and SmolVLA reproduction kit. VLA-Scratch
[
weng2026vlascratch
]
provides a modular, performance-oriented training stack built on PyTorch FSDP2 with support for multiple VLM backbones (Qwen3-VL, PaliGemma, SmolVLM), heterogeneous dataset co-training, and Hydra-based configuration for rapid experimentation.
StarVLA
[
community2026starvla
]
further advances this direction by explicitly decoupling backbone architectures from action heads and supports both VLM backbones (e.g., Qwen-VL) and world-model backbones (e.g., Cosmos) with multiple options for action heads (autoregressive tokens, continuous decoding, and flow-matching), and integrates multiple benchmarks through a unified evaluation interface. Dexbotic
[
xie2025dexbotic
]
takes an experiment-centric approach, adopting a unified PyTorch toolbox with optimized reimplementations of various VLAs across different platforms such as the Franka and SO-101.
3
VLA Foundry
VLA Foundry
is an open-source framework for training LLMs, VLMs, and VLAs within a single codebase. It is designed around end-to-end control of the embodied-model pipeline: the same training loop, data abstractions, and configuration interface extend from language pretraining to vision-language training and action learning. In this sense, VLA Foundry connects capabilities often treated separately across LLM/VLM training frameworks
[
megatron-lm
,
olmo20242olmo2furious
,
karamcheti2024prismatic
]
and VLA frameworks
[
kim2024openvla
,
physicalintelligence2025openpi
,
gr00tn1_2025
,
cadene2024lerobot
,
weng2026vlascratch
,
community2026starvla
]
. For robotics, this unified stack makes it practical to build and scale VLA systems while exploring new training recipes, architectures, and data mixtures. It supports both pre-training from scratch or initialization from pretrained Hugging Face backbones without requiring users to switch codebases across stages. An accompanying tutorial illustrates the full LLM
→
\rightarrow
VLM
→
\rightarrow
VLA training path from scratch
1
1
1
https://github.com/TRI-ML/vla_foundry/tutorials/training_llm_vlm_vla.ipynb
.
In this section we present the key elements of the VLA Foundry framework that we believe make it a useful tool for policy pretraining research and experimentation.
3.1
Design Principles
VLA Foundry is designed around four principles. We state them here; Section
3.2
shows how the architecture embodies each, and Appendix
6
gives the full reference.
1.
Modularity and Composability
– Components plug together rather than being baked into the training code. Models, data pipelines, encoders, and loss handlers are instantiated by name from a YAML-based configuration system that supports nested includes, so presets can be composed, locally overridden, and reused across experiments; swapping a vision encoder, a language backbone, or an entire model type is a single command-line change.
2.
Hackability and Interoperability
– Any component can be extended or replaced without touching the rest of the system. We avoid heavy framework wrappers (PyTorch Lightning, Hugging Face Trainer) and keep the training loop thin with parallelism primitives exposed rather than hidden, so users are not locked into a particular stack and can extend the framework with new modeling architectures or distributed-training paradigms as they emerge.
3.
Performance
– VLA Foundry targets researchers with moderate to medium-scale compute. Training throughput has been benchmarked across LLM, VLM, and VLA stages up to 128 GPUs across 16 nodes.
4.
Reproducibility
– Runs are repeatable at a given configuration. We rely on deterministic per-rank RNG seeding, dataloader state checkpointing for exact restarts, and immutable frozen dataclasses that prevent hidden configuration changes at runtime.
3.2
Framework
VLA Foundry’s architecture has four layers: a YAML-based configuration system backed by frozen dataclasses, a registry that makes models and data pipelines pluggable, modality-specific preprocessing and dataloading, and a model-agnostic training loop. The remainder of this section walks through each; Appendix
6.1
gives the full reference.
3.2.1
Modular Configuration System
VLA Foundry’s modularity and composability is ensured by our configuration system. We base it on Draccus
[
draccus
]
: every parameter is declared in a dataclass and can be overridden by a YAML preset or a command-line argument, in increasing order of priority. Presets themselves are composable – a YAML file can inherit from others, so experiments are expressed by combining building blocks rather than by duplicating them. Parameters shared across modules (e.g., hidden dimensions, sequence lengths) are resolved once and propagated through the dataclass tree preventing silent cross-module mismatches. Configuration dataclasses are frozen to avoid run-time configuration changes that easily result in discrepancies between configuration files, logs, and runtime. See Appendix
6.1.2
for details and a worked example.
3.2.2
Extending the Framework
VLA Foundry is designed to be hackable and extensible. Adding a model that fits an existing model type (LLM, VLM, or DP-VLA) requires only a parameter dataclass and a factory function, registered by name at import time; the model type’s
batch handler
– which owns batching, loss construction, and output reduction – is shared, and a single training loop drives all model types. A new batch handler is needed only when introducing a new training paradigm.
Adding a dataset follows a similar pattern. Raw data is converted to WebDataset
[
breuel2020webdataset
]
tar shards through a per-modality preprocessing stage. Preprocessing runs in parallel with Ray
[
moritz2018ray
]
and emits both training shards and the per-dataset statistics needed for normalization at training time.
The dataloading pipeline itself is an ordered composition of small stages that users extend or reorder independently from the training loop. Dataloader can be mixed and each dataset contributes its own shards, statistics, and modalities with weighted dataset proportions.
3.2.3
Robotics Data Handling
Robotics data carries structure beyond what text and image-caption pipelines handle. Normalization is known to require careful handling in multi-dataset robotics training
[
lbmtri2025
]
; our
RoboticsNormalizer
supports global and per-timestep schemes, including percentile-based variants. Statistics can be merged across datasets. For percentile estimation and merging, we use t-digest
[
dunning2019computing
]
. Actions may be represented in absolute world-frame coordinates or relative to an anchor end effector pose, with rotations in the 6D continuous format
[
zhou2019continuity
]
and relative poses composed in SE(3). Actions are chunked
[
Zhao-RSS-23
]
in a configurable window of past and future time steps around an anchor: the future portion supervises the model, the past portion is available as input. Proprioceptive observations are causally restricted to past and current time steps. See Appendix
6.2
.
3.2.4
Training Performance
The training loop supports the standard levers for scaling distributed training – FSDP (with optional CPU offloading), mixed precision, gradient checkpointing,
torch.compile
, and gradient accumulation. See Appendix
6.1.1
.
Figure
2
shows the training throughput across the different stages of our pipeline (LLM, VLM, and VLA). We used a 1.2 billion parameter language model, add a 86 million parameter ViT for the VLM and add a 325 million parameter transformer action head for the VLA. For the LLM, we used a sequence length of 2048 tokens with padding if needed. For the VLM, each image is represented with 64 tokens and the caption inputs are variable lengths but for consistency, we chose a total length of 256 tokens, truncated and padded sequences as needed. For the VLA, the model encodes 8 images from different cameras and timesteps, producing 512 tokens and a short task description. We pad VLA sequences dynamically and the average sequence length is 549 tokens. At this model scale, each GPU can hold the full model weights during training thus FSDP doesn’t offer an advantage, and even shows weaker scaling for the VLM.
Figure 2
:
Throughput scaling as the number of GPU nodes is increased for the LLM, VLM, and VLA with either DDP or FSDP parallelization. Tests were done through Sagemaker on P5 nodes of 8 Nvidia H100 GPUs each. See section
4
for details about the models.
3.3
Evaluation
VLA Foundry supports evaluation on
lbm_eval_oss
, the open-source release of the
LBM
simulation benchmark
[
lbmtri2025
]
. The
lbm_eval_oss
framework is a challenging benchmark that uses the high fidelity Drake physics engine
[
drake
]
to model the robots and scene dynamics. It defines 49 tasks to measure the performance of table top bimanual manipulation policies. Users can compare their own trained policies against the released checkpoints under a shared protocol. We ship the simulator as a Docker image, sidestepping platform-specific build and dependency issues across user environments. A simple dashboard lets users manage evaluation experiments, view rollout videos, and plot results as they accumulate.
Additionally, we provide rigorous statistical analysis via STEP
[
snyder2025step
]
to compare success rates of multiple policies. Following
[
lbmtri2025
,
lin2026systematic
,
lerobot_unfolding_robotics
]
, the dashboard has violin plots for Bayesian estimates of individual success rates, with Compact Letter Display (CLD)
[
piepho2004algorithm
]
attached for comparison. Policies not sharing any CLD alphabet are significantly different at 5% family-wise error rate (FWER). Notably, our statistical framework lets the user base decisions on intermediate comparisons, as results are gathered. The user can decide to stop an evaluation early to save time, or to collect more rollouts than initially planned to seek higher statistical power. Such a practice would constitute harmful “p-hacking”
[
stefan2023big
]
for standard statistical tests such as Barnard’s test
[
barnard_significance_1947
]
. More details can be found in prior work
[
snyder2025step
,
tri_2026_ab_testing
,
lin2026systematic
]
; we include our general design principles and suggested best practices as documentation in the dashboard. In particular, when concatenating results over multiple tasks for aggregate comparison, we balance the per-task sample size for each policy to ensure that the aggregate represents an unbiased estimate of the policy’s equally-weighted multi-task performance. For instance, if some Model A has
[
50
,
49
,
50
,
50
]
[50,49,50,50]
rollouts across 4 tasks, where the second task is missing one rollout, the results are truncated to
[
49
,
49
,
49
,
49
]
[49,49,49,49]
before aggregation. Therefore, Model A’s aggregated performance is computed by 196 rollouts instead of 199 before it is fed to STEP for comparison. We note that this unbiased aggregation was not strictly enforced in the prior work
[
lbmtri2025
]
. Our results as well as those from
[
lbmtri2025
]
are included in the dashboard so that users can compare their own experiments with the reported numbers from the released checkpoints.
4
Foundry-VLA-1.7B
and
Foundry-Qwen3VLA-2.1B-MT
Having described the framework itself, we now turn to two applications. We release two VLA models types alongside this report. Each showcase different capabilities of the VLA Foundry pipeline:
•
Foundry-VLA-1.7B
– trained fully from scratch along the LLM
→
\rightarrow
VLM
→
\rightarrow
VLA pipeline, demonstrating end-to-end controllability over the training recipe.
•
Foundry-Qwen3VLA-2.1B-MT
– trained on top of a pretrained Qwen3-VL 2B backbone, showing that the same codebase efficiently supports the traditional VLM
→
\rightarrow
VLA recipe and that a stronger/larger VLM backbone translates into a more capable VLA.
Both models share the same action expert architecture (Section
4.1
). Section
4.1
walks through the from-scratch pipeline, Section
4.2
describes the Qwen3-based model, and Section
4.3
reports simulation results, including ablations over multi-task vs. single-task training as well as sim-only and real-only subsets.
4.1
Foundry-VLA-1.7B
: Training From Scratch
Foundry-VLA-1.7B
is our end-to-end demonstration of VLA Foundry’s full-pipeline controllability. We first train a language model (LLM), extend it to a vision-language model (VLM), and finally adapt it into a vision-language-action (VLA) model (Figure
1
). We release the intermediate
Foundry-LLM-1.2B
and
Foundry-VLM-1.3B
checkpoints in addition to
Foundry-VLA-1.7B
so that the community can reproduce or modify any stage of the pipeline
2
2
2
https://huggingface.co/collections/TRI-ML/vla-foundry
.
LLM training
We used a standard transformer architecture
[
open_lm
]
to define a 1.2 billion parameter model with a hidden dimension of 2048, 24 layers, and 16 heads. Note that, following the convention
[
kaplan2020scaling
]
, we discount the additional 200 million parameters of the embedding layers.
The model was trained on 500 million samples (or 1 trillion tokens) from the openly available DCLM
[
li2024datacomp
]
dataset with a sequence length of 2048. Text was tokenized with the processor
HuggingFaceTB/SmolVLM2-256M-Video-Instruct
, which has a vocabulary size of 49,280.
We used a warmup-stable-decay learning rate schedule
[
hu2024minicpm
]
. The model and its full set of configuration parameters is available on HuggingFace
2
. Table
1
shows results of this model on standard benchmarks before the learning rate decay phase and after the full training. Note the lack of instruction tuning and the size of the model keep it close to random chance on difficult benchmarks such as MMLU; however, we see good results well above random chance on easier benchmarks.

VLM training
We add a 86 million parameter randomly initialized vision transformer (ViT)
[
dosovitskiy2021imageworth16x16words
]
, with a similar architecture as CLIP
[
radford2021learning
]
, to encode (
224
×
224
224\times 224
) input images. A pixel-shuffle
[
marafioti2025smolvlm
]
, operation is used as pooling to reduce the sequence length of the image.
We assemble the ViT and pooling with the previously pre-trained 1.2B LLM at 800B tokens of training – before the learning rate cooldown, following recommendations from
[
keh2025should
]
. The VLM is trained with 200M samples of the openly available DataCompDR-1B
[
gadre2023datacomp
]
3
3
3
Image links from this dataset are known to break, which limits exact reproducibility.
. Our results are reported in Table
2
as evidence of end-to-end training functionality rather than as a claim of optimal performance. We also include qualitative examples in Figure
3
.
Although in this instance we use a randomly initialized ViT and the in-house LLM, both could instead be replaced by off-the-shelf pre-trained components such as SigLIP
[
zhai2023sigmoid
]
or DINO
[
oquab2023dinov2
,
simeoni2025cijo
]
which would likely lead to improved model performance. Alternatively, the VLM itself can take advantage of pre-trained backbones such as PaliGemma2

VLA training
We define the VLA architecture on top of the previous VLM (Figure
4
). To extend the VLM architecture to to predict robot actions, we begin by adding a new
observation
token to the LLM vocabulary. The VLM input sequence is composed of images and a text describing a task as well as the new observation token, in that order. The embedded sequence that is fed to the LLM part of the VLM is composed of the concatenated embedded image patches from multiple images, embedded text tokens, and the embedded observation token.
The hidden features of the last
N
N
(in our experiments, 4) layers of the VLM matching the observation token are used to condition a flow transformer that denoises an action sequence. This action head is a 325 million parameter transformer with the same architecture as the LLM (except a vocabulary size of 0). Its input sequence is composed of the concatenated hidden features from the VLM, optionally, the proprioception encoded with a linear layer and the noised action sequence also encoded by a linear layer, in that order. The output action sequence is trained with the flow-matching objective
[
lipman2022flow
]
. We denote this model
Foundry-VLA-1.7B
.
Figure 4
:
Foundry-VLA-1.7B
architecture. Four images over two timesteps each are fed into the same ViT image encoder. For each of the 8 images, the result is pooled with “pixel-shuffle”
[
marafioti2025smolvlm
]
(see appendix
8.4
) and projected into the embedding space of the LLM. An additional observation token is appended to the sequence. The LLM embedding of the last layer matching this token is passed to a flow transformer with a noised action sequence. The flow transformer outputs the predicted denoising direction.

A key design principle of VLA Foundry is that architectural components can be swapped with minimal effort. To exercise this, we also train a VLA with the pretrained Qwen3-VL 2B model

5
Conclusions
Limitations
This initial release reflects deliberate choices in scope rather than framework constraints. Our reported evaluation is restricted to closed-loop LBM simulation on a narrow slice of embodiments, and we do not yet include real-hardware numbers; VLA Foundry’s shared evaluation and dataloader abstractions are designed so that additional simulation suites (e.g., LIBERO, SimplerEnv, RoboCasa), new embodiments, and on-robot evaluation can be added without touching the core training stack. All experiments in this report use a flow-matching action head; while additional heads such as a diffusion policy are already implemented in the codebase, the action head is a modular component and integrating further variants – for example, autoregressive discrete action tokenizations – requires only a new head module rather than changes to the training loop or data pipeline. Finally, although VLA Foundry exposes the full LLM–VLM–VLA pipeline with probabilistic multi-modal mixing, we do not yet characterize optimal data recipes across stages, nor do we address safety, alignment, or failure-mode detection for embodied agents. We view these as open research directions that VLA Foundry is well-positioned to enable, and we invite the community to build on it to explore them.
Conclusion
In this technical report, we introduced
VLA Foundry
, an open-source framework that unifies LLM, VLM, and VLA training within a single codebase. The framework provides end-to-end control over the embodied-model pipeline – from language pretraining through action learning – with shared abstractions for data, configuration, training, and evaluation. Alongside the framework, we released two model types:
Foundry-VLA-1.7B
, trained fully from scratch through the LLM
→
\rightarrow
VLM
→
\rightarrow
VLA pipeline, and
Foundry-Qwen3VLA-2.1B-MT
, built on a pretrained Qwen3-VL backbone with the same action head and training recipe.
On closed-loop LBM evaluation,
Foundry-VLA-1.7B-MT-sim
is statistically on par with our prior closed-source LBM performance over our 16-tasks benchmark.
Foundry-Qwen3VLA-2.1B-MT
outperforms both models with a wide margin of 23 percentage points on average. We demonstrated that VLA Foundry can be used to build VLAs both from-scratch and starting with a pretrained-backbone model. Together with the released checkpoints, the statistical comparison dashboard, and integration of
lbm_eval_oss
, VLA Foundry’s unified LLM–VLM–VLA stack enables the community to explore the design space that connects these stages – training recipes, multi-modal data mixing, fusion architectures – within a single codebase. We hope these tools will serve the community and that the community will contribute to their improvements.

6.1
Framework Internals
6.1.1
Model Training Loop
Training in VLA Foundry is done in a single training loop that is shared across every model stage in the pipeline: LLM pretraining, VLM pretraining, and VLA fine-tuning. VLA Foundry takes a data-centered approach. It expresses training budgets as a number of samples instead of a number of steps, so that runs at different batch sizes or GPU counts remain directly comparable.
The training loop is deliberately model-agnostic. Unpacking a batch into inputs and targets, masking the loss, and reducing model outputs to a scalar is attached to each model class rather than baked into the loop, so the same training pathway drives an LLM learning from raw text, a VLM learning from image–caption pairs, and a VLA learning to denoise actions. The loop composes cleanly with the distributed training primitives users expect: FSDP with optional CPU offloading, mixed-precision execution, gradient accumulation, gradient checkpointing,
torch.compile
, and exponential moving average of weights.

6.1.6
Preprocessing and Manifests
VLA Foundry has custom scripts to convert raw datasets to the WebDataset tar shards described above. As noted in Section
6.1.4
, we currently support text, image-caption, and robotics datasets. Text preprocessing reads parquet files (typically stored on S3) and emits one JSON sample per row. Image-caption preprocessing takes a URL list and downloads image–text pairs via
img2dataset
. Utilities are also shipped for fetching upstream data from Hugging Face Hub and from HTTP directory listings into the intermediate storage consumed by these scripts. Robotics raw data can come from any source (simulation logs, real-robot recordings, etc.), as long as a converter knows how to read it and produce a standardized output; for this release, we provide converters from LeRobot and from the Spartan format used in
lbm_eval
. These robotics converters all share the same entry point and follow the same logic, so adding a new one amounts to creating a new class that inherits from
BaseRoboticsConverter
and filling in the necessary methods such as
discover_cameras
.
We use
ray
[
moritz2018ray
]
to parallelize data preprocessing. Under the
ray
framework, there is a head node that orchestrates the jobs and several worker nodes that each run a small independent job. For robotics datasets, this is done in multiple stages. First, it creates a
frames
folder in the output directory, where each sample is its own unique tar file. Next, it creates an
episodes
folder, where the sample tar files are grouped together by episode. Finally, it creates a
shards
folder, where sample tar files are grouped together randomly. This
shards
folder is what is ultimately used for training. Within the
shards
folder, there is a
manifest.json
which contains an overview of the shards; an example follows.

Robotics datasets additionally have a
stats.json
within the
shards
folder, which contains statistics computed across all samples of the dataset. This computation requires worker nodes to first store local statistics in node memory, then communicate and gather across different nodes. For internal runs, this was tested on AWS EC2 with 60 nodes of
i4i.4xlarge
, but we have tested it locally as well.
6.2
Robotics-specific Details
6.2.1
Normalization
Actions and proprioceptive states are normalized during dataloading time and denormalized at inference time. Normalization is handled by a
RoboticsNormalizer
class, which supports four normalization methods: standard deviation, min-max, and two percentile-based variants (
percentile_1_99
and
percentile_5_95
). The choice of percentile-based normalization is useful for action fields that contain outliers, as it avoids compressing the bulk of the distribution to a narrow band. Statistics are precomputed across the full dataset during preprocessing and stored in a
stats.json
file alongside each dataset shard.
Normalization scope
Normalization can be applied at two scopes. In
global
scope, the scalars
mean
and
scale
are applied uniformly across all timesteps in a sequence. In
per-timestep
scope, each timestep within the action window has its own mean and scale derived from statistics computed at that relative offset in the trajectory. Per-timestep normalization is particularly useful for relative action representations, where the distribution of predicted displacements can vary considerably between early and late steps of the prediction horizon. When working with cropped sequences (see Section
6.2.3
), per-timestep statistics are aligned to the anchor timestep so that indices into the statistics tensor correspond correctly to the tensor’s time axis.
Merging statistics
When training on multiple datasets simultaneously (Section
6.1.5
), users may wish to use the joint distribution across all datasets rather than any individual one. Since datasets are processed individually with their own per-dataset
stats.json
files, we support merging multiple
stats.json
files together. Means are computed as sample-count-weighted averages. Standard deviations are merged using the law of total variance,

[

during preprocessing, and the sketches are merged at training time to recover approximate percentiles of the pooled distribution. All statistics are computed and merged per action-space dimension, and optionally per timestep within the prediction window when using per-timestep normalization scope.

preview_text:
We present VLA Foundry, an open-source framework that unifies LLM, VLM, and VLA training in a single codebase. Most open-source VLA efforts specialize on the action training stage, often stitching together incompatible pretraining pipelines. VLA Foundry instead provides a shared training stack with end-to-end control, from language pretraining to action-expert fine-tuning. VLA Foundry supports both from-scratch training and pretrained backbones from Hugging Face. To demonstrate the utility of our framework, we train and release two types of models: the first trained fully from scratch through our LLM-->VLM-->VLA pipeline and the second built on the pretrained Qwen3-VL backbone. We evaluate closed-loop policy performance of both models on LBM Eval, an open-data, open-source simulator. We also contribute usability improvements to the simulator and the STEP analysis tools for easier public use. In the nominal evaluation setting, our fully-open from-scratch model is on par with our prior closed-source work and substituting in the Qwen3-VL backbone leads to a strong multi-task table top manipulation policy outperforming our baseline by a wide margin. The VLA Foundry codebase is available at https://github.com/TRI-ML/vla_foundry and all multi-task model weights are released on https://huggingface.co/collections/TRI-ML/vla-foundry. Additional qualitative videos are available on the project website https://tri-ml.github.io/vla_foundry.

## 论文详细摘要

### 1. 研究背景和动机
当前机器人基础模型发展迅速，但大多数开源VLA（视觉-语言-动作）框架仅专注于动作训练阶段，将上游的LLM和VLM预训练视为固定或无关的环节。这种分离导致研究人员无法系统探索从语言预训练到策略学习的完整设计空间。同时，机器人交互数据严重稀缺，非机器人数据的重要性日益增长，亟需一个统一的框架来支持端到端的可控训练。

### 2. 核心方法和技术创新
本文提出**VLA Foundry**，一个开源框架，在单一代码库中统一了LLM、VLM和VLA训练。其核心创新包括：
- **全流水线可控性**：通过共享的配置驱动接口，支持从语言预训练到动作专家微调的每个阶段干预。
- **灵活的多模态训练**：支持文本、图像-描述和机器人数据的概率性混合，精确控制训练配比。
- **原生Hugging Face集成**：可轻松加载预训练骨干网络（如Qwen3-VL）。
- **模块化设计**：模型、数据管道、编码器等组件可插拔，通过YAML配置即可切换。

### 3. 主要实验结果
- **Foundry-VLA-1.7B**（从零训练）：在LBM仿真基准上，其多任务版本与先前闭源工作LBM性能相当。
- **Foundry-Qwen3VLA-2.1B-MT**（基于Qwen3-VL骨干）：在16个任务上平均成功率比LBM高出**23个百分点**，展现出更强骨干网络带来的显著优势。
- 多任务训练和微调策略在强骨干模型上效果更佳，而弱骨干模型上单任务训练反而更好。

### 4. 研究意义和价值
VLA Foundry为机器人策略预训练研究提供了一个**统一、可复现、可扩展**的实验平台。它使研究人员能够系统探索数据配方、架构设计和训练策略之间的相互作用，降低了从零构建VLA系统的门槛。所有代码、模型权重和评估工具均已开源，有望推动社区在机器人基础模型领域的协作与创新。

qa_pairs.question: 这篇论文的核心创新点是什么，他想解决什么问题，怎么解决的？
qa_pairs.answer:
## 核心创新点

**VLA Foundry** 的核心创新在于提出了一个**统一的、端到端的开源框架**，将 **LLM（大语言模型）、VLM（视觉语言模型）和 VLA（视觉语言动作模型）** 的训练整合在同一个代码库中，实现了从语言预训练到动作专家微调的**全流程可控性**。

## 要解决的问题

1. **现有框架碎片化**：大多数开源 VLA 工作只专注于动作训练阶段，往往需要拼接不兼容的预训练流水线，导致研究灵活性受限。
2. **数据稀缺与异构性**：机器人交互数据严重不足，且与语言/视觉数据的分布差异大，但现有框架很少能灵活混合多模态数据。
3. **缺乏端到端控制**：LLM/VLM 预训练阶段的数据决策会直接影响下游机器人性能，但现有工具无法让研究者对整个流水线进行统一干预。

## 解决方案

### 1. 统一训练栈
- **共享数据加载和训练循环**：从 LLM → VLM → VLA 的每个阶段都使用相同的训练循环、数据抽象和配置接口。
- **支持从零训练和预训练骨干**：既可以从头训练，也可以加载 Hugging Face 上的预训练模型（如 Qwen3-VL）。

### 2. 模块化与可扩展设计
- **YAML 配置系统**：基于 Draccus，支持嵌套、继承和命令行覆盖，轻松切换模型架构、数据流水线和训练策略。
- **注册机制**：通过 `@register_model` 和 `@register_batch_handler` 装饰器，添加新模型或训练范式无需修改核心代码。
- **灵活的数据混合**：支持文本、图像-描述和机器人数据的概率性混合，通过 `--data.dataset_weighting` 精确控制训练配比。

### 3. 机器人数据处理专用工具
- **归一化**：支持全局/每时间步的多种归一化方案（标准差、分位数等），并支持跨数据集合并统计量（使用 t-digest 合并分位数）。
- **动作表示**：支持绝对/相对动作，6D 连续旋转表示，以及可配置的过去/未来动作窗口。
- **本体感知**：仅使用过去和当前时间步的本体感知信息，符合因果结构。

### 4. 高性能分布式训练
- 基于 **FSDP2** 和云原生工具（AWS SageMaker、S3），支持多节点多 GPU 训练，自动梯度累积、混合精度和检查点同步。
- 在 128 个 H100 GPU 上进行了基准测试。

### 5. 评估与分析工具
- 集成 **LBM Eval** 仿真基准（49 个任务），提供 Docker 镜像简化部署。
- 使用 **STEP** 统计工具进行严格的成功率比较，支持贝叶斯估计、紧凑字母显示（CLD）和中间停止评估（避免 p-hacking）。

## 实际验证

论文通过两种模型展示了框架的有效性：

| 模型 | 方法 | 性能 |
|------|------|------|
| **Foundry-VLA-1.7B** | 完全从零训练（LLM→VLM→VLA） | 与之前闭源的 LBM 模型持平 |
| **Foundry-Qwen3VLA-2.1B-MT** | 基于预训练 Qwen3-VL 骨干 | 比 LBM 基线高出 **23 个百分点** |

## 总结

VLA Foundry 通过**统一框架、模块化设计、灵活数据混合和端到端控制**，解决了 VLA 研究中流水线碎片化、数据异构和缺乏可复现性的问题，为机器人基础模型的研究提供了一个可扩展、可复现的实验平台。

qa_pairs.question: 基于他的前作，梳理这个方向的整个发展脉络，每一步相比于之前的工作都改进了什么，着重于几个不同的发展方向。
qa_pairs.answer:
好的，作为一名专业的学术论文分析助手，我将基于您提供的论文《VLA Foundry: A Unified Framework for Training Vision-Language-Action Models》，梳理其所在领域（即视觉-语言-动作模型，VLA）的发展脉络，并分析每一步相比之前工作的改进，着重于几个不同的发展方向。

## 发展脉络与改进分析

VLA（Vision-Language-Action）模型旨在让机器人通过理解视觉和语言指令来执行物理动作。该领域的发展可以看作是从“拼接式”的独立模块，向“统一化”、“可控制”的端到端框架演进的过程。VLA Foundry 的出现，标志着这一演进达到了一个新的高度。

### 1. 早期探索：从独立模型到初步整合

这个阶段的核心是将预训练的语言模型（LLM）和视觉模型（VLM）与动作模块进行拼接。

-   **代表工作**:
    -   **OpenVLA** (Kim et al., 2024): 这是早期里程碑式的工作，它将一个预训练的VLM（基于Prismatic框架）与一个简单的动作头（action head）拼接起来，通过微调来学习机器人动作。
    -   **π0 (Pi-zero)** (Physical Intelligence, 2025): 通过其开源框架 **OpenPi** 提供，训练了一个大规模的VLA模型，并强调了在大量机器人数据（超过10,000小时）上进行预训练的重要性。
    -   **GR00T** (NVIDIA, 2025): 采用“双系统”架构，将视觉-语言骨干网络与一个扩散Transformer动作头结合。

-   **核心改进**:
    -   **从无到有**：证明了将强大的VLM与动作学习结合起来的可行性，为VLA领域奠定了基础。
    -   **数据驱动**：强调了大规模、多样化的机器人数据对于训练通用策略的重要性。

-   **主要局限**:
    -   **拼接式架构**：这些框架通常专注于“动作训练”阶段，而将上游的LLM/VLM预训练视为一个固定的、不可控的黑盒。不同阶段的训练代码、数据管道和配置往往是分离的，导致研究灵活性受限。
    -   **算法耦合**：代码库与特定的算法决策（如特定的VLM架构、动作头设计）紧密耦合，难以进行模块化的实验和探索。

### 2. 标准化与基础设施：降低门槛与提升可复现性

这个阶段的工作重点从“创造新模型”转向“创造更好的工具”，旨在标准化VLA的训练流程，降低研究门槛。

-   **代表工作**:
    -   **LeRobot** (Cadene et al., 2024): 采用社区优先的方法，专注于廉价硬件（如SO-100机械臂），集成了数据收集、训练和部署的全流程，并训练了轻量级模型 **SmolVLA**。
    -   **VLAb** (Aubakirova et al., 2025): 作为LeRobot的补充，提供了一个专门的预训练库和SmolVLA的复现工具包。
    -   **VLA-Scratch** (Weng et al., 2026): 提供了一个模块化、高性能的训练栈，基于PyTorch FSDP2，支持多种VLM骨干网络（如Qwen3-VL, PaliGemma）和异构数据集协同训练。
    -   **StarVLA** (Community, 2026): 进一步解耦了骨干网络架构和动作头，支持多种VLM和世界模型骨干，以及多种动作头（自回归、连续解码、流匹配）。

-   **核心改进**:
    -   **标准化与模块化**：这些框架开始强调模块化设计，允许用户更灵活地更换模型组件（如视觉编码器、语言模型、动作头）。
    -   **降低门槛**：通过提供统一的接口、配置系统和数据管道，使得研究人员可以更容易地复现、比较和改进不同的VLA方法。
    -   **性能优化**：引入了更高效的分布式训练策略（如FSDP2），使得在有限的计算资源下训练更大模型成为可能。

-   **主要局限**:
    -   **仍聚焦于VLA阶段**：尽管在VLA训练阶段实现了模块化，但大多数框架仍然将LLM和VLM的预训练视为外部过程。用户如果想要探索从语言预训练开始的完整数据配方和训练策略，仍然需要切换不同的代码库。
    -   **数据孤岛**：缺乏一个统一的框架来管理不同模态（文本、图像-文本对、机器人数据）的混合和协同训练，这限制了研究人员探索不同数据来源对最终机器人策略性能影响的能力。

### 3. 统一与全流程控制：VLA Foundry 的突破

VLA Foundry 的出现，代表了该领域向“全流程可控”迈出的关键一步。它不再是一个简单的VLA训练框架，而是一个**统一的LLM、VLM和VLA训练平台**。

-   **代表工作**: **VLA Foundry** (Mercat et al., 2026, 本文)

-   **核心改进与创新点**:

    1.  **全流程统一与可控性 (End-to-End Control)**:
        -   **改进**: 解决了之前所有工作的核心痛点。VLA Foundry 将LLM预训练、VLM预训练和VLA微调整合在**同一个代码库、同一个训练循环、同一套数据抽象和配置系统**中。
        -   **价值**: 研究人员现在可以端到端地控制整个训练配方，从语言数据的配比、视觉编码器的选择，到动作头的设计，都可以在一个统一的框架下进行实验，无需拼接不同工具。这为探索“数据缩放律”、“骨干网络预训练策略”等根本性问题提供了前所未有的工具。

    2.  **灵活的模态混合与数据加载 (Flexible Multi-modal Training)**:
        -   **改进**: 原生支持文本、图像-文本和机器人数据的**概率性混合**。通过简单的YAML配置，用户可以精确控制每个训练阶段的数据混合比例。
        -   **价值**: 解决了机器人数据稀缺的瓶颈。研究人员可以系统性地研究如何利用海量的语言和视觉数据来弥补机器人数据的不足，探索最优的跨模态数据配方。

    3.  **模块化与可扩展性 (Modularity and Hackability)**:
        -   **改进**: 采用基于YAML的配置系统和注册机制，使得添加新的模型架构、数据集或训练范式变得非常简单，无需修改核心代码。例如，从 `Foundry-VLA-1.7B`（从头训练）切换到 `Foundry-Qwen3VLA-2.1B-MT`（使用预训练骨干），只需更改配置文件。
        -   **价值**: 极大地提升了研究效率。研究人员可以快速迭代和比较不同的架构设计，例如更换不同的视觉编码器（ViT, SigLIP, DINO）或动作头（流匹配、扩散策略）。

    4.  **从零训练与预训练骨干的无缝支持 (From-Scratch vs. Pre-trained)**:
        -   **改进**: 同时支持从零开始训练整个LLM-VLM-VLA流水线，以及直接加载Hugging Face上的预训练VLM骨干（如Qwen3-VL）。
        -   **价值**: 提供了两种互补的研究路径。前者让研究人员拥有对模型训练的完全控制权，后者则允许他们利用最先进的VLM能力，快速构建高性能的机器人策略。论文实验表明，更强的VLM骨干（Qwen3-VL）直接带来了更强的VLA性能（`Foundry-Qwen3VLA-2.1B-MT` 大幅超越 `Foundry-VLA-1.7B`）。

    5.  **严谨的评估与统计分析 (Rigorous Evaluation)**:
        -   **改进**: 集成了开源的仿真基准 `lbm_eval_oss` 和先进的统计分析工具 `STEP`，提供贝叶斯成功率估计和紧凑字母显示（CLD）进行多策略比较。
        -   **价值**: 提供了比简单比较平均成功率更科学、更可靠的评估方法，有助于得出更稳健的结论，避免了“p-hacking”等问题。

### 总结：不同发展方向

基于以上分析，我们可以将VLA领域的发展脉络归纳为以下几个关键方向：

| 发展方向 | 早期探索 (OpenVLA, π0) | 标准化与基础设施 (LeRobot, VLA-Scratch) | 全流程统一 (VLA Foundry) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **核心目标** | 证明VLA可行性，构建基础模型 | 降低门槛，提升可复现性和模块化 | 实现全流程可控，探索根本性研究问题 |
| **训练控制** | 仅控制VLA微调阶段 | 控制VLA训练阶段，模块化组件 | **控制LLM→VLM→VLA全流程** |
| **数据管理** | 数据管道分离，难以混合 | 支持多数据集，但模态混合有限 | **原生支持多模态概率性混合** |
| **架构灵活性** | 架构固定，难以更换组件 | 模块化设计，可更换部分组件 | **高度模块化，可轻松更换任何组件** |
| **评估方法** | 简单成功率比较 | 标准化基准，但统计方法较简单 | **集成先进统计分析工具，科学

qa_pairs.answer:
好的，根据您提供的论文《VLA Foundry: A Unified Framework for Training Vision-Language-Action Models》，我将分析其与前作的关系。需要说明的是，这篇论文本身是一个**框架**和**模型**的发布，其“前作”主要指的是论文中直接引用的、来自同一研究机构（Toyota Research Institute, TRI）或紧密相关的先前工作，特别是**LBM**。 以下表格详细对比了VLA Foundry与其关键前作LBM，以及其他在论文中被提及的、具有代表性的相关模型。 ### 与前作LBM的对比 | 对比维度 | **前作: LBM (LBM-TRI-2025)** | **本文: VLA Foundry** | **主要改进/区别** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **核心贡献** | 发布了一个**闭源的**、高性能的多任务桌面双臂操作策略模型。 | 发布了一个**开源的**、统一的**训练框架**，以及基于该框架训练的两个**开源模型**。 | **从“发布模型”转变为“发布框架+模型”**。核心贡献从单一模型转向了可复现、可扩展的研究基础设施。 | | **开源程度** | **闭源**。模型权重、代码、训练细节未公开。 | **完全开源**。代码、模型权重、训练配置、数据预处理脚本全部公开。 | **根本性改变**。解决了前作不可复现、不可修改的问题，极大地降低了社区研究和复用的门槛。 | | **框架范围** | 专注于**VLA训练阶段**。模型架构是定制的，与上游LLM/VLM训练流程不兼容。 | **统一了LLM、VLM、VLA训练**。提供从语言预训练到动作微调的端到端控制。 | **从“单阶段”扩展到“全流水线”**。允许研究者探索数据配方、骨干网络预训练对最终机器人策略的影响，这是前作无法做到的。 | | **模型架构** | 使用预训练的CLIP模型作为视觉和文本编码器，搭配一个**交叉注意力**的扩散Transformer动作头。 | 使用**自回归VLM**（如Qwen3-VL或自训练的VLM）作为骨干，搭配一个**流匹配（Flow Matching）** Transformer动作头。 | **架构更现代、更灵活**。VLA Foundry的架构允许利用更强大的VLM骨干网络，并且动作头是可替换的模块。 | | **骨干网络** | 固定使用CLIP。 | 支持多种骨干网络，包括**从零训练的VLM**和**Hugging Face上的预训练VLM**（如Qwen3-VL）。 | **灵活性大幅提升**。用户可以根据计算资源和性能需求，自由选择和替换视觉语言骨干。 | | **数据使用** | 使用了包括**开源OXE数据**在内的更大数据集。 | 主要使用**内部收集的仿真和真实数据**（与LBM大部分重叠，但略多），**未使用OXE数据**。 | **数据配方更可控**。通过不使用外部开源数据，VLA Foundry的实验能更清晰地归因于模型和框架本身，而非数据差异。 | | **性能表现** | 在LBM仿真基准上表现强劲，是当时的SOTA。 | **Foundry-VLA-1.7B** (从零训练) 与LBM性能**持平**。
**Foundry-Qwen3VLA-2.1B-MT** (基于Qwen3-VL) 性能**大幅超越LBM**（平均高出23个百分点）。 | **性能显著提升**。证明了利用更强VLM骨干（Qwen3-VL）的有效性，同时验证了从零训练的模型也能达到前作水平。 | | **评估工具** | 使用标准统计测试。 | 集成了**STEP**分析工具，提供更严谨的贝叶斯估计和多重比较校正（CLD），并支持**中间停止评估**。 | **评估方法论更先进**。提供了更科学、更高效的策略比较方式，避免了“p-hacking”问题。 | | **仿真环境** | 使用闭源的`lbm_eval_cs`。 | 使用并改进了**开源的`lbm_eval_oss`**，并提供了Docker镜像。 | **仿真环境开源化**。解决了环境复现的难题，使社区可以在完全相同的条件下进行公平比较。 | ### 与其他相关前作的对比 除了LBM，论文还提到了其他一些重要工作，但VLA Foundry与它们的关系更多是**借鉴与整合**，而非直接迭代。 | 前作 | VLA Foundry的主要区别与改进 | | :--- | :--- | | **OpenVLA** | OpenVLA是早期开源VLA的代表，但其代码库基于Prismatic，主要关注VLA微调。VLA Foundry则提供了从LLM到VLA的**完整流水线**，并且架构更灵活，支持流匹配等新范式。 | | **OpenPI / GR00T** | 这些是特定模型的训练框架，与模型本身耦合紧密。VLA Foundry是**模型无关的**，旨在成为一个通用的、可扩展的框架，而非特定模型的配套代码。 | | **VLA-Scratch / StarVLA** | 这些是同期或稍晚的VLA训练框架。VLA Foundry的独特优势在于其**统一的LLM-VLM-VLA流水线**和**对从零训练的完整支持**，而其他框架可能更侧重于VLA阶段的优化或特定架构。 | ### 总结：VLA Foundry的主要改善 1. **从闭源到开源**：这是最根本的转变，使得研究可复现、可验证、可扩展。 2. **从单阶段到全流水线**：统一了LLM、VLM、VLA的训练，让研究者能控制整个数据与训练配方，探索更广泛的设计空间。 3. **从固定架构到模块化框架**：支持多种骨干网络（从零训练或预训练）、多种动作头（流匹配、扩散策略），通过YAML配置即可切换，极大提升了灵活性。 4. **从单一模型到框架+模型**：不仅发布了更强的模型（Foundry-Qwen3VLA-2.1B-MT），更重要的是提供了一个强大的研究工具，赋能整个社区。 5. **评估体系的科学化**：集成了STEP分析工具和开源仿真环境，使得策略评估更严谨、更公平、更高效。

好的，作为专业的学术论文分析助手，我将对《VLA Foundry: A Unified Framework for Training Vision-Language-Action Models》这篇论文进行深入分析，并按照您的要求，以表格形式详细列出其关键技术方法、数学原理和具体参数。 ### 论文核心要点分析 **VLA Foundry** 是一个开源的、统一的框架，旨在解决当前机器人基础模型（VLA）训练中存在的碎片化问题。其主要技术创新和实际价值体现在： 1. **统一流水线 (Unified Pipeline)**：将LLM（大语言模型）、VLM（视觉语言模型）和VLA（视觉语言动作模型）的训练整合在同一个代码库中，提供了从语言预训练到动作微调的端到端控制。这解决了以往不同阶段需要拼接不同工具的问题。 2. **模块化与可配置性 (Modularity & Configurability)**：通过基于YAML的配置系统和组件注册机制，用户可以轻松地更换模型架构、数据集、编码器等组件，而无需修改核心训练代码。这极大地提高了研究灵活性和实验效率。 3. **灵活的机器人数据处理 (Flexible Robotics Data Handling)**：针对机器人数据特有的结构（如动作归一化、相对/绝对动作表示、时间窗口等）提供了专门的工具和抽象，使得多数据集混合训练和数据处理更加便捷和鲁棒。 4. **从零训练与预训练骨干支持 (From-Scratch & Pretrained Backbone Support)**：框架既支持从零开始训练完整的LLM->VLM->VLA流水线，也支持直接加载Hugging Face上的预训练VLM骨干（如Qwen3-VL），展示了其强大的兼容性。 5. **严格的统计评估 (Rigorous Statistical Evaluation)**：集成了LBM仿真基准和STEP统计分析工具，提供了超越简单平均成功率的、具有统计显著性的模型性能比较方法。 --- ### 关键技术方法、数学原理与参数表格 以下表格详细列出了论文中提出的关键技术方法，并尽可能包含了数学原理推导和具体参数。 | 技术方法 | 技术细节与数学原理 | 具体参数与配置 | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **1. 统一训练框架** | **原理**：设计一个模型无关的训练循环，通过 `BatchHandler` 抽象来解耦模型架构与训练逻辑。不同模型（LLM, VLM, VLA）共享同一个训练循环，但各自拥有独立的 `BatchHandler` 来处理数据打包、损失计算和输出规约。 | - **训练循环**：以样本数（samples）而非步数（steps）定义训练预算，确保不同批次大小和GPU数量下的实验可比性。
- **并行策略**：支持FSDP（全分片数据并行，可选CPU卸载）、混合精度、梯度累积、梯度检查点、`torch.compile` 和指数移动平均（EMA）。 | | **2. 模块化配置系统** | **原理**：基于 `Draccus` 库，所有参数在冻结的数据类（dataclass）中声明。配置优先级为：命令行参数 > YAML预设文件 > 数据类默认值。YAML文件支持 `include` 关键字进行嵌套和组合。 | - **示例**：`python main.py --config_path config.yaml --model.hidden_dim=1024`
- **`config.yaml` 结构**：
```yaml
model:
include: vla_foundry/config_presets/models/transformer_11m.yaml
hidden_dim: 2048
vit:
include: vla_foundry/config_presets/models/vit_paligemma.yaml
hparams:
lr: 1e-4
global_batch_size: 256
``` | | **3. 组件注册机制** | **原理**：通过 `@register_model` 和 `@register_batch_handler` 装饰器，将模型工厂函数和批处理句柄注册到全局注册表中。运行时，`create_model()` 根据 `model.type` 参数从注册表中查找并实例化对应的模型。 | - **注册新模型**：只需定义一个冻结的数据类和一个带 `@register_model("diffusion_policy")` 装饰器的工厂函数。
- **注册新批处理句柄**：通过 `@register_batch_handler` 为新的训练范式（如扩散策略）注册句柄。 | | **4. 数据集混合与加载** | **原理**：
1. **数据格式**：使用 WebDataset 格式，将数据打包成 tar 分片，支持多模态数据（文本、图像、动作）。
2. **混合策略**：通过 `--data.dataset_weighting` 参数（如 `1:2:1`）控制不同数据集的采样比例。
3. **数据加载管道**：每个模态（文本、图像描述、机器人）定义独立的、可组合的 WebDataset 处理管道。 | - **数据目录结构**：
```
dataset_name/
manifest.jsonl
shard_00000000.tar
unique_name_1_camera1.jpg
unique_name_1_actions.npz
...
```
- **混合配置示例**：
```yaml
dataset_manifest: [task1/manifest.jsonl, task2/manifest.jsonl]
dataset_weighting: [1.0, 2.0]
``` | | **5. 机器人数据归一化** | **原理**：`RoboticsNormalizer` 类支持多种归一化方法，并处理多数据集统计信息的合并。
1. **归一化方法**：
- **标准差归一化**: `x_normalized = (x - mean) / std`
- **最小-最大归一化**: `x_normalized = (x - min) / (max - min)`
- **百分位数归一化**: `x_normalized = (x - p_low) / (p_high - p_low)`，其中 `p_low` 和 `p_high` 是低百分位和高百分位（如1%和99%）。
2. **统计信息合并**：
- **均值**: 按样本数加权平均。
- **标准差**: 使用总方差公式 `σ²_overall = E[σ²_i]`。
- **百分位数**: 使用 t-digest 算法合并多个数据集的百分位数草图。 | - **归一化范围**：
- **全局 (Global)**: 对所有时间步使用相同的均值和尺度。
- **每时间步 (Per-timestep)**: 对预测窗口内的每个时间步使用独立的均值和尺度。
- **配置**：通过 `--action_fields` 和 `--proprioception_fields` 指定需要归一化的字段。 | | **6. 动作表示与时间窗口** | **原理**：
1. **绝对/相对动作**：
- **绝对动作**: 世界坐标系下的末端执行器位姿。
- **相对动作**: 相对于锚点时间步（anchor timestep）的末端执行器位姿。数学上，若 `T_ref` 是锚点位姿，`T_t` 是未来时间步 `t` 的位姿，则相对动作为 `T_t_rel = T_ref⁻¹ · T_t`。
2. **旋转表示**：使用6D连续旋转表示法 `[zhou2019continuity]`，通过Gram-Schmidt正交化在 `SO(3)` 矩阵和6D表示之间转换。
3. **时间窗口**：每个训练样本围绕一个锚点时间步 `t` 构建，窗口范围为 `[t - N_past, t + N_future]`，产生 `N_past + 1 + N_future` 个时间步。 | - **配置参数**：
- `past_lowdim_steps` (N_past): 过去时间步数。
- `future_lowdim_steps` (N_future): 未来时间步数。
- `action_fields`: 指定动作字段（如 `["pose_6d", "gripper"]`）。
- `proprioception_fields`: 指定本体感受字段（如 `["joint_positions", "joint_velocities"]`）。
- **本体感受限制**：仅使用窗口内的过去和当前时间步（`[0, t_anchor]`），体现因果性。 | | **7. VLA架构 (Foundry-VLA-1.7B)** | **原理**：
1. **图像编码**：使用ViT将图像编码为图像块（patch），并通过“像素重排”（pixel-shuffle）操作进行池化，减少序列长度。

好的，根据您提供的论文内容，以下是关于该论文使用的评价指标与数据集的详细分析。 ## 评价指标与数据集细节 该论文主要使用**仿真环境**中的**任务成功率**作为核心评价指标，并辅以**统计显著性检验**来确保比较的可靠性。数据集方面，论文使用了自有的**仿真和真实机器人数据**，并引用了公开的**语言和视觉语言数据集**进行预训练。 ### 1. 评价指标 论文的核心评价指标是**任务成功率**，并在此基础上进行了深入的统计分析。 | 评价指标 | 细节与说明 | | :--- | :--- | | **任务成功率 (Task Success Rate)** | - **定义**：在仿真环境中，针对特定任务，模型成功完成的 rollout 次数占总 rollout 次数的比例。
- **评估预算**：每个模型-任务组合的标准评估预算为 **200 次 rollout**。
- **评估平台**：主要在 `lbm_eval_oss` (开源版本) 和 `lbm_eval_cs` (闭源版本) 两个仿真基准上进行。 | | **统计显著性检验 (STEP)** | - **目的**：严谨地比较不同策略的成功率，判断差异是否具有统计显著性。
- **方法**：使用 **STEP** 分析工具，生成小提琴图 (violin plots) 展示成功率的贝叶斯估计分布。
- **结果呈现**：通过**紧凑字母显示法 (CLD)** 标记策略。不共享相同字母的策略在 **5% 的族系错误率 (FWER)** 下被认为存在显著差异。
- **聚合比较**：在进行多任务聚合比较时，会平衡每个任务的样本量，确保聚合结果是无偏的。 | | **语言模型基准 (LLM Benchmarks)** | - **目的**：评估从零训练的 LLM (`Foundry-LLM-1.2B`) 的语言能力。
- **指标**：在多个选择题推理基准上的准确率。
- **具体基准**：HellaSwag, MMLU, ARC-e, ARC-c, PIQA, WinoGrande, OpenBookQA, BoolQ。 | | **视觉语言模型基准 (VLM Benchmarks)** | - **目的**：评估 VLM (`Foundry-VLM-1.3B`) 的视觉-语言能力。
- **指标**：在 COCO Captions 验证集上的图像描述生成质量。
- **具体指标**：BLEU-1, BLEU-2, BLEU-3, BLEU-4, ROUGE-L, CIDEr。 | ### 2. 数据集 论文使用了多阶段、多来源的数据集，涵盖了从语言预训练到机器人策略微调的完整流程。 | 数据集类别 | 数据集名称/来源 | 用途与细节 | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **语言预训练 (LLM)** | **DCLM** | - **用途**：从零训练 `Foundry-LLM-1.2B`。
- **规模**：5亿样本（约1万亿 tokens），序列长度为 2048。 | | **视觉语言预训练 (VLM)** | **DataCompDR-1B** | - **用途**：在 `Foundry-LLM-1.2B` 基础上训练 `Foundry-VLM-1.3B`。
- **规模**：2亿样本。 | | **机器人策略训练 (VLA)** | **LBM 自有数据集** | - **来源**：论文团队先前工作 `LBM` 中使用的内部数据，包括仿真和真实遥操作演示。
- **构成**：
- **仿真数据**：42个任务，7,548个 episodes。
- **真实数据**：361个任务，47,068个 episodes。
- **总计**：403个任务，54,616个 episodes。
- **训练样本**：预处理后共生成约 **1880万** 个训练样本。
- **注意**：与 `LBM` 论文相比，数据集略有不同（例如，排除了开放数据集 OXE 和 UMI 数据）。 | | **仿真评估 (Simulation Eval)** | **lbm_eval_oss / lbm_eval_cs** | - **用途**：评估 VLA 策略的闭环性能。
- **任务**：
- **已见任务 (Seen Tasks)**：16个，用于多任务训练和评估。
- **未见任务 (Unseen Tasks)**：3个，用于测试零样本泛化能力。

qa_pairs.question: 论文在哪些数据集上进行了实验？主要的评估指标和性能提升是多少？
qa_pairs.answer:
## 论文实验数据集、评估指标与性能提升

### 实验数据集

论文主要在以下数据集上进行了实验：

- **LLM 训练**：使用 **DCLM** 数据集（500 million samples，约 1 trillion tokens）
- **VLM 训练**：使用 **DataCompDR-1B** 数据集（200 million samples）
- **VLA 训练**：使用内部收集的 **LBM** 数据集，包含：
  - **仿真数据**：42 个任务，7,548 个 episodes
  - **真实机器人数据**：361 个任务，47,068 个 episodes
  - **总计**：403 个任务，54,616 个 episodes

### 主要评估指标

论文使用 **LBM Eval** 仿真基准进行闭环策略评估，包含：
- **16 个训练时见过的任务**（seen tasks）
- **3 个训练时未见过的任务**（unseen tasks）
- 每个任务评估 **200 个 rollout episodes**

统计方法使用 **STEP** 框架，通过 **贝叶斯估计** 和 **Compact Letter Display (CLD)** 进行显著性检验（5% family-wise error rate）。

### 性能提升

#### 1. 与基线模型 LBM 的比较（图 5）

| 模型 | 性能对比 |
|------|----------|
| **Foundry-VLA-1.7B-MT-sim** | 与 LBM-MT **统计上持平** |
| **Foundry-Qwen3VLA-2.1B-MT** | **大幅超越** LBM-MT，平均提升 **23 个百分点** |

#### 2. LLM 基准测试（表 1）

| 基准 | 训练 800B tokens | 训练 1T tokens |
|------|------------------|----------------|
| HellaSwag | 64.3% | **66.7%** |
| MMLU | 26.0% | 26.6% |
| ARC-e | 70.3% | **71.7%** |

qa_pairs.question: 论文的主要局限性有哪些？未来可能的改进方向是什么？
qa_pairs.answer:
## 论文的主要局限性

根据论文内容，主要局限性包括：

- **评估范围有限**：论文的评估仅限于 LBM 仿真环境中的闭环策略，未包含真实机器人硬件上的评估结果。这限制了结论在真实世界中的泛化性。

- **动作头类型单一**：所有实验均使用流匹配（flow-matching）动作头，虽然代码库中已实现扩散策略等其他动作头，但论文未对这些变体进行系统比较。

- **数据配方未优化**：论文虽然暴露了完整的 LLM→VLM→VLA 训练管线，但未系统性地探索不同阶段的最优数据混合比例、数据规模与模型性能之间的关系。

- **安全性、对齐与故障检测缺失**：论文未涉及具身智能体的安全性、对齐或故障模式检测等关键问题。

- **仿真环境版本差异**：由于仿真环境从闭源版本（`lbm_eval_cs`）到开源版本（`lbm_eval_oss`）的更新，模型性能存在显著差异，开源版本可被视为分布偏移后的版本，这增加了结果复现和比较的复杂性。

## 未来可能的改进方向

- **扩展评估范围**：
  - 集成更多仿真套件（如 LIBERO、SimplerEnv、RoboCasa）
  - 在真实机器人硬件上进行评估，验证框架在真实场景中的有效性

- **丰富动作头类型**：
  - 系统比较流匹配、扩散策略、自回归离散动作标记化等多种动作头
  - 探索不同动作头在不同任务类型中的适用性

- **优化数据配方**：
  - 系统研究 LLM、VLM、VLA 各阶段的最优数据混合策略
  - 探索非机器人数据（语言、视觉数据）对下游机器人策略性能的影响
  - 研究数据规模与模型性能的缩放规律

- **安全性研究**：
  - 引入安全对齐机制
  - 开发故障模式检测与恢复策略

- **提升框架可用性**：
  - 提供更多预训练骨干网络的支持
  - 简化新数据集和新任务类型的集成流程
  - 完善文档和教程，降低使用门槛

- **社区协作**：
  - 鼓励社区贡献新的模型架构、数据集和评估基准
  - 建立标准化的比较协议，促进可重复性研究
